covered_sections: 4.2 Polymerization Reactions>Addition Polymerization>Steps of addition polymerization

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: What distinguishes an oligomer from a true polymer?

options.A: Oligomers have fewer than 100 monomer units; polymers have more
options.B: Oligomers are always branched; polymers are always linear
options.C: Oligomers are synthetic; polymers are natural

questions.hint: Oligo- means few; poly- means many.
questions.explanation: An oligomer typically contains fewer than 100 monomer units, while a polymer generally has more than 100 repeating units. Option B is false (both can be branched or linear), and oligomers can be natural or synthetic.

source_trace.content_anchor: structures with lower numbers of monomers are known as oligomers

explanations_tiered.definition: An oligomer is a short-chain polymer with fewer than 100 monomer units.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Polymers generally have >100 monomers; oligomers have fewer. Oligomers may be intermediates in polymerization.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Oligomers are like short trains (few cars); polymers are like long trains (many cars).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The degree of polymerization (DP) for oligomers is typically less than 100, while true polymers have DP > 100.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Oligomers often exhibit different physical properties than high molecular weight polymers, such as lower viscosity and different solubility.
misconceptions: Oligomers are always unnatural? Some natural oligomers exist (e.g., oligosaccharides).
Oligomers cannot polymerize further? They can be intermediates.

glossary_entry.definition: Short polymer chain with few repeating units.

flashcard.front: What is the difference between an oligomer and a polymer?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Oligomers have fewer than 100 monomer units; polymers have more.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: A short polymer with fewer than 100 monomer units.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Oligomers have fewer than 100 monomer units.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: What initiates a free radical polymerization reaction?

options.A: A molecule that decomposes to form free radicals
options.B: A cation that attacks a monomer's double bond
options.C: An anion that transfers an electron to the monomer

questions.hint: Free radicals are produced by initiator decomposition.
questions.explanation: Free radical polymerization is initiated by an initiator molecule that decomposes (often by heat or light) to generate free radicals. These radicals then attack monomer double bonds. Cations and anions initiate ionic polymerizations.

source_trace.content_anchor: polymerization reaction initiated by free radicals is called a free radical polymerization

explanations_tiered.definition: Free radical polymerization is initiated by a molecule that decomposes to form free radicals.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Initiators such as benzoyl peroxide or AIBN decompose upon heating or UV exposure to produce radicals that add to monomers.
explanations_tiered.beginner: An initiator molecule breaks apart into unpaired electrons (free radicals) to start the reaction.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The initiator undergoes homolytic cleavage to form radicals. These radicals then attack the \(\pi\)-bond of a vinyl monomer, starting chain growth.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Common free radical initiators have weak O-O or N=N bonds. The bond dissociation energy determines the rate of radical formation.
reasoning_steps: Free radical polymerization requires free radicals.
Free radicals come from initiator decomposition.
The initiator is not a cation or anion.
misconceptions: Free radicals are stable? No, they are highly reactive.
Any heat alone initiates polymerization? Some monomers need initiators.

glossary_entry.definition: Molecule that decomposes to form radicals to start polymerization.

flashcard.front: What starts a free radical polymerization?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Decomposition of an initiator molecule into free radicals.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Decomposition of an initiator into free radicals.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Molecule that produces radicals to start polymerization.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Free radical initiators decompose to form radicals.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: What is the reactive centre in cationic polymerization?
questions.explanation: Cationic polymerization is initiated by a cation (positively charged ion), which attacks the electron-rich double bond of an unsaturated monomer. Free radicals initiate free radical polymerization, and anions initiate anionic polymerization.

source_trace.content_anchor: polymerization reaction initiated by a cation is called a cationic polymerization

explanations_tiered.definition: Cationic polymerization uses a positively charged ion (cation) as the reactive centre.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Cations such as \(H^+\) or carbocations attack electron-rich monomers (e.g., alkenes with electron-donating groups) to initiate chain growth.
explanations_tiered.beginner: In cationic polymerization, the 'starter' is a positive ion that attacks the monomer.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The cation adds to the double bond, creating a new carbocation at the chain end, which can add more monomers.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Cationic polymerization requires monomers with electron-donating substituents (e.g., -OR, -Ph) to stabilize the propagating carbocation.
reasoning_steps: The reactive centre carries a positive charge.
misconceptions: Cationic polymerization uses free radicals? No, that is free radical polymerization.
Cations are always metal ions? They can be carbocations too.

glossary_entry.definition: Addition polymerization initiated by a cation.

flashcard.front: What is the reactive centre in cationic polymerization?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What charge does the reactive centre have in cationic polymerization?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with anionic (negative) and free radical (neutral).
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Cationic polymerization uses a positively charged ion.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which type of initiator is used in anionic polymerization?
questions.explanation: Anionic polymerization is initiated by an anion (negatively charged species), such as an organolithium compound. The anion attacks the monomer's double bond, creating a new carbanion at the chain end.

source_trace.content_anchor: polymerization reaction initiated by an anion is called an anionic polymerization

explanations_tiered.definition: Anionic polymerization is initiated by a negatively charged ion (anion).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Anions such as \(OH^-\), \(CN^-\), or organolithium compounds (\(RLi\)) attack electron-deficient monomers (e.g., acrylates) to start polymerization.
explanations_tiered.beginner: An anionic polymerization starts with a negative ion that attacks the monomer.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The anion adds to the monomer, creating a new carbanion at the chain end. This carbanion can then add more monomers.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Anionic polymerization is 'living' when no chain termination occurs, allowing precise control over molecular weight and block copolymers.
misconceptions: Anionic polymerization uses free radicals? No.
Anions are always in solution? They can be nucleophiles.

glossary_entry.definition: Addition polymerization initiated by an anion.

flashcard.front: What type of initiator is used in anionic polymerization?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What charge does the initiator have in anionic polymerization?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with cationic (+ve) and free radical (neutral).
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Anionic polymerization uses an anion as initiator.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: How does combination termination differ from chain transfer in free radical polymerization?

options.A: Combination joins two radicals; chain transfer passes the radical to another molecule
options.B: Combination requires an initiator; chain transfer does not
options.C: Combination adds a monomer; chain transfer removes a monomer
options.D: Combination is irreversible; chain transfer is reversible

questions.hint: Combination links two chains; chain transfer moves the active centre.
questions.explanation: In combination termination, two growing radical chains link together, destroying both radicals. Chain transfer transfers the radical to another molecule (monomer, solvent, or polymer), allowing that molecule to continue propagation.

source_trace.content_anchor: Termination is achieved by combination reaction when two chains link or chain transfer

explanations_tiered.definition: Combination joins two radical chains; chain transfer moves the radical to another species.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Combination terminates two chains simultaneously. Chain transfer does not terminate; it shifts the active centre to another molecule, which can continue polymerization.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Combination is like two trains crashing and stopping; chain transfer is like passing the driver's seat to another train.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: In combination, two radical chain ends combine via covalent bond formation. In chain transfer, the radical is abstracted by another molecule (e.g., solvent), which then becomes the new radical.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Chain transfer lowers molecular weight but does not stop polymerization if the new radical can reinitiate. Transfer agents (mercaptans) are used to control molecular weight.
reasoning_steps: Combination: two radicals meet and bond → termination.
Chain transfer: radical moves to another molecule → new radical continues.
Chain transfer does not terminate overall polymerization.
misconceptions: Chain transfer always stops polymerization? No, it may continue if the new radical is reactive.
Combination produces two polymers? It produces one from two.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Combination: two chains link; Chain transfer: radical moves.

glossary_entry.definition: Transfer of radical centre to another molecule.

flashcard.front: How does combination differ from chain transfer?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Combination joins two radicals; chain transfer moves the radical to another molecule.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the difference between combination and chain transfer?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Combination terminates two chains; chain transfer moves the radical to another molecule.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Combination = termination; chain transfer = radical relocation.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use analogy: handshake vs passing a baton.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Combination joins two radicals; chain transfer passes the radical.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: During free radical polymerization, the rate constant for propagation (\(k_p\)) is \(2.5 \times 10^3 \text{ L mol}^{-1} \text{s}^{-1}\) and the radical concentration is \(1.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ mol/L}\). The monomer concentration is 5.0 mol/L. What is the rate of propagation?

explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Propagation rate depends on monomer concentration and radical concentration. It is the rate at which monomer is consumed in chain growth.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Multiply the three numbers: the propagation constant, the monomer amount, and the radical amount.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This rate expression assumes steady-state radical concentration. \(k_p\) is typically much larger than \(k_t\) (termination), and high molecular weight polymers form rapidly.
worked_solution: Step 1: Write propagation rate equation: \(R_p = k_p [M][R^\bullet]\)
misconceptions: Forgetting to multiply all three factors.
Using wrong units (k_p in different units).

glossary_entry.definition: Rate of monomer consumption during chain growth.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Formula for propagation rate in free radical polymerization?

D.subject: Chemistry
D.question: Nylon 66 is made from hexamethylenediamine (\(\mathrm{H_2N(CH_2)_6NH_2}\), molar mass = 116.2 g/mol) and adipic acid (\(\mathrm{HOOC(CH_2)_4COOH}\), molar mass = 146.1 g/mol). Each pair of monomers loses one water molecule (18.0 g/mol) to form a repeat unit. What is the molar mass of the repeat unit in Nylon 66?

D.hint: Add both monomer masses, then subtract the mass of water lost.
D.explanation: Repeat unit mass = (116.2 + 146.1) - 18.0 = 262.3 - 18.0 = 244.3? Wait recompute: 116.2+146.1=262.3; minus 18.0 = 244.3. But that is the diad. Option B (226.3) is incorrect. Actually 262.3-18=244.3, which is option A. Correction: The repeat unit in Nylon 66 has two monomer residues minus one water per amide bond. Two amide bonds form? No, one repeat unit contains two monomers minus two waters? Let me recalc properly. One molecule of diamine + one molecule of diacid forms a dimer with one water loss. That dimer has mass 116.2+146.1-18=244.3 g/mol. That is the correct repeat unit for the polymer chain (the repeating segment). So correct answer is A. But my initial mental math was wrong. I will correct the answer to A.

explanations_tiered.definition: Repeat unit mass = sum of monomer masses minus mass of water eliminated.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For each pair of monomers, one water molecule is lost, so repeat unit mass = 116.2 + 146.1 - 18.0 = 244.3 g/mol.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Add the two monomer weights, then subtract the weight of the water molecule that leaves.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The repeat unit in Nylon 66 contains residues of both monomers minus H₂O: 116.2 + 146.1 - 18.0 = 244.3 g/mol.
explanations_tiered.advanced: For a polyamide, each amide bond formation eliminates one water. The repeat unit is -NH-(CH₂)₆-NH-CO-(CH₂)₄-CO- with molar mass 244.3 g/mol.
worked_solution: Step 3: Total mass before condensation = 262.3 g/mol
Step 4: Subtract mass of water lost = 18.0 g/mol
reasoning_steps: Each repeat unit comes from one diamine + one diacid minus one H₂O.
misconceptions: Forgetting to subtract water (giving 262.3).
Subtracting two waters (226.3) — that is for two amide bonds but one repeat unit has only one water loss per monomer pair.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Nylon repeat mass = diamine + diacid - H₂O.

glossary_entry.definition: Smallest repeating segment in a polymer chain.

flashcard.front: Calculate Nylon 66 repeat unit mass: diamine 116.2, diacid 146.1, water 18.0.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How to find repeat unit mass of a condensation polymer?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Sum of monomer masses minus mass of byproduct.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Smallest repeating part of a polymer.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Remind students to account for one water per linkage.

D.subject: Chemistry
D.question: What is the formula for the repeat unit in polypropylene?
D.explanation: Polypropylene is an addition polymer of propylene (\(\mathrm{CH_2=CHCH_3}\)). After polymerization, the double bond becomes a single bond, yielding repeat unit \(-\mathrm{CH_2-CH(CH_3)}-\) with the methyl group as a side chain.

source_trace.content_anchor: The monomer of polypropylene is propylene (propene) CH₂=CHCH₃

explanations_tiered.definition: Polypropylene repeat unit is \(-\mathrm{CH_2-CH(CH_3)}-\) derived from propylene.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Propylene (\(\mathrm{CH_2=CHCH_3}\)) undergoes addition polymerization, opening the double bond to form a chain with pendant methyl groups.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Polypropylene has a backbone of -CH₂-CH- with a methyl (CH₃) sticking out.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The repeat unit in polypropylene is \(-\mathrm{CH_2-CH(CH_3)}-\) because propene adds via its double bond.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Polypropylene is a stereoregular polymer; isotactic, syndiotactic, and atactic forms exist due to methyl group orientation.
misconceptions: Polypropylene is the same as polyethylene? No, polyethylene has no methyl side groups.
Propylene repeat unit is -CH₂-CH₂-? That is polyethylene.

glossary_entry.definition: Addition polymer of propene, repeat unit -CH₂-CH(CH₃)-.

flashcard.front: What is the repeat unit of polypropylene?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the structural repeat unit of polypropylene?
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Polypropylene repeat unit is -CH₂-CH(CH₃)-.

D.subject: Chemistry
D.question: The monomer styrene has the formula \(\mathrm{C_6H_5CH=CH_2}\). What is the structure of the repeat unit in polystyrene?
D.hint: Addition polymerisation of vinyl monomers gives -CH₂-CHR- repeat unit.
D.explanation: Styrene polymerises via addition, opening the double bond. The repeat unit becomes \(-\mathrm{CH_2-CH(C_6H_5)}-\) with the phenyl ring as a side group on every other carbon.

source_trace.content_anchor: polymerization of styrene molecules gives polystyrene

explanations_tiered.definition: Polystyrene repeat unit is \(-\mathrm{CH_2-CH(C_6H_5)}-\) from styrene monomer.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Styrene (\(\mathrm{C_6H_5CH=CH_2}\)) polymerises via the vinyl double bond, placing the phenyl ring on every other backbone carbon.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Polystyrene has a -CH₂-CH- backbone with a benzene ring attached to every other carbon.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Addition polymerisation of styrene opens the double bond, yielding \(-\mathrm{CH_2-CH(C_6H_5)}-\) as the repeat unit.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Polystyrene is atactic in commercial forms, making it amorphous and transparent. The bulky phenyl rings restrict chain rotation, raising the glass transition temperature.
misconceptions: Polystyrene contains double bonds? No, all carbons are sp³ in the backbone.
Phenyl group is on every carbon? No, on alternating carbons.

glossary_entry.definition: Addition polymer of styrene, repeat unit -CH₂-CH(C₆H₅)-.

flashcard.front: What is the repeat unit of polystyrene?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the structural repeat unit of polystyrene?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show expanded structure with phenyl ring.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. The repeat unit is -CH₂-CH(C₆H₅)-.

D.subject: Chemistry
D.question: What is the correct condensed structural formula for the repeat unit of polyvinyl chloride (PVC)?
D.hint: PVC monomer is vinyl chloride: CH₂=CHCl.
D.explanation: PVC is made from vinyl chloride monomer (\(\mathrm{CH_2=CHCl}\)). Addition polymerisation opens the double bond, giving repeat unit \(-\mathrm{CH_2-CHCl}-\) with chlorine on alternate carbons.

explanations_tiered.definition: Polyvinyl chloride repeat unit is \(-\mathrm{CH_2-CHCl}-\) from vinyl chloride.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Vinyl chloride (\(\mathrm{CH_2=CHCl}\)) undergoes addition polymerisation to form PVC with a chlorine atom on every other carbon.
explanations_tiered.beginner: PVC has a chain of -CH₂-CHCl- repeating, with chlorine atoms sticking out.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The repeat unit in PVC is \(-\mathrm{CH_2-CHCl}-\) because the double bond in \(\mathrm{CH_2=CHCl}\) becomes a single bond during polymerisation.
explanations_tiered.advanced: PVC is atactic in commercial grades, leading to amorphous regions. The chlorine atoms provide polarity, increasing interchain forces and strength.
misconceptions: PVC contains double bonds? No.
PVC repeat unit is -CHCl-CHCl-? That would require monomer CHCl=CHCl (1,2-dichloroethene).

glossary_entry.definition: Polyvinyl chloride, addition polymer of vinyl chloride.

flashcard.front: What is the repeat unit of polyvinyl chloride?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the structural repeat unit of PVC?
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. PVC repeat unit is -CH₂-CHCl-.
